tokens: As Apollo 8 moves around behind the Moon, Jim prepares for a series of tracking exercises.
By the Flight Plan, this will require him sighting on a landmark on the far side known as Control Point 2 which is situated in the vicinity of crater Keeler.
His subsequent comm after AOS shows that he also sights on Control Point 1, a keyhole-shaped pair of craters within Korolev.
Using an adapter, he will attach the 16-mm movie camera to the sextant so that it can simultaneously film the sightings.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Part of the mission objectives for Apollo 8 is to demonstrate the ability of the spacecraft's optics and computer to determine the position of a landmark.
Up to this time, much of the Moon's far side had been photographed by the Lunar Orbiter probes though the photography provided could not allow accurate determination of positions of surface features.
Jim will use the computer to approximately aim the optics at the landmark.
He will then aim them accurately and take marks.
Using its knowledge of their orbit, the computer will calculate where it thinks the landmark really is with three parameters; latitude, longitude and altitude.
Note that their computer is not programmed to deal directly with longitude.
Instead it was programmed to work with longitude divided by two so that the limited range of its registers can deal with longitude to the required precision.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Jim's figures represent the computer's idea of where CP-1 is.
This is a small crater next to a keyhole-shaped feature in Korolev.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Keyhole is quite distinctive as shown by this image from Bill's stereo sequence.
There are spaces on page 2-59 of the Flight Plan for Jim to write down the position of CP-1.
By looking up these coordinates in the Clementine Lunar Atlas, it appears Jim's mark is very close, much more so than the coordinates given in the Flight Plan which appears to be 140 km to the west.
Subsequent comm will show that Jim has decided to choose his own marks for the tracking exercise rather than use those given in the Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: These are the computer's figures for Jim's mark on Control Point-2.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Control Point-2 is a small crater in the north rim of the 37-km crater Planté, itself within the larger crater Keeler.
Planté was names after physicist Gustav Planté (1834-1889).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 77 hours, 32 minutes into the flight.
All quiet for approximately the last 20 minutes.
We are due to acquire again in about 13 minutes.

One of the more interesting system phenomena, I guess we could call it, to come out of these early revolutions around the Moon is the temperature variance we're seeing within the Environmental Control System.
The system is proving quite capable to the task, but it's seeing much wider excursions than it sees in Earth orbital flight.

And I'm talking about temperatures on the variances - excursions, I guess is the proper word, of 40 to 50 degrees within the system; that's not in the cabin, of 40 to 50 degrees, whereas the same - at the same point in Earth orbital flight between the light and dark side, might see an excursion on the order of 10 to 15 degrees.
Again, the Environmental Control System is handling the cabin very nicely.
It's been purposely set up somewhat warmer than yesterday and some previous days the crew prefers the cabin up in the higher 70's and that's where it has been on consistently today, 77 to 79.
But again and again, we hear action on the water boiler as we come around from the dark side into the Sun side.
This phenomena I'm sure, will be examined at more length at the change of shift briefing, and as the flight progresses.
No new conversation to report, all's well at 77 hours, 34 minutes into the flight.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Looks like the evaporat - looks like the evaporator is holding okay, or at least it's trying to.
It dropped the temperature down to about 32 and now it's come back up again and stabilized at about 42 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: What we're doing on the control point tracking, I managed to look for a CP-1 at the same time we were trying to do a CP-2 on this rev.
I've picked two marks which are just as small, but more easily recognizable, than the ones that were given to me.
I know that I can repeat the process in picking the same small point on the next rev.
Now I can try to look for the control points that are written down, but I think that I have better control over the ones that we have.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Jim's comm implies that he has chosen points like Keyhole to suit himself rather than using ones which are chosen for him.
As long as he can reference them after the flight.
this should not be a problem.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 77 hours, 49 minutes into the flight and just three minutes ago we acquired Apollo 8 as it came around the corner on this fifth revolution around the Moon.
Here's the conversation as it progresses.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: One more point: the control point times which you've given me are a little bit off, and I can notice, by comparing the maps, that these maps are not too well aligned either.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Roger, these two small points that you can repeat your marks on: will you be able to identify those precisely on a map?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: That's affirmative; that's why I picked them.
They're both - they're very prominent features, and they are both very small craters about the same size as the ones we are looking for, but I can pinpoint them on a map.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: One more comment: is that, an awful lot of controversy at data priority meetings, it looks like 10 degrees pitch-up is the best attitude to obtain the horizon so that you can follow the landmark down through the scanning telescope.
If you pitch down any more, a full-up trunnion will not gather, or get the horizon, and the horizon's a great help in leading yourself into the control point.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Jim, we concur with your use of the two small craters which you can repeatably mark on and find on the map; and also, if you'll give us your new latitudes and longitudes, we can compute for you a time of closest approach to those points with the spacecraft 10 degrees pitched up.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: CP-1, latitude, minus 6 - 06269; longitude over 2, minus 78954; altitude, plus 00152; for CP-2, latitude, minus 09638; longitude over 2, plus 81691; altitude, minus 00007.
I tried to get CP-3 at the same pass, but I let it go by to get set up for this first crack at the landing site.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: On CP-1, would you say again the latitude, and on CP-2, say again the longitude over 2, please.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: CP-1, latitude, minus 06269 - that's the latitude; and for longitude over 2 for CP-2, plus 81691.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: And it appears that Resolve, Medium is a very good combination to track.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: And it appears so far, Houston, that no spacecraft pitch motion is required to get 5 marks on the target in plenty of time.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Understand you require no spacecraft motion to get 5 marks.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: And that brings us up to this point.
We've had no additional comments now, for perhaps a minute or so.
The kind of - precisely the kind of data that we had hoped to get.
The navigational side of lunar orbit which will be flown by both the command - future command module and LM flights around the Moon.
Lovell will continue to his navigational work; and dear old Bill Anders, sitting off in one corner there, squeezing off pictures at a rate that most professional photographers wouldn't believe.
Frank Borman is entering a rest period here, which is to extend for three hours.
It's doubtful that he'll really go to sleep, but he has been excellent about following the Flight Plan to date - he probably needs rest.
At 77 hours, 57 minutes into the flight; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I am about 15 minutes early with the TEI-6 update and the map update.
I'll have them here whenever it's convenient for you to copy.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We'd like to ask you to stop using auto optics on the pseudo-landing site.
It's necessary that we send you up a P27 to update the RLS values stored in the computer.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: I found out it didn't work [garble] I went to manual optics on B-1.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: If you'd go to P00 and Accept, please, we're going to send you a P27 load which will update an RLS value which will be followed by a procedural change, Jim, we'll give you later; and auto optics should be working shortly.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Or I could use no-landmark Auto Optics instead of the code.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: We're also sending you up a state vector update at the same time.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We taking the DSE for a dump.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We would like to take Bill's DSE for a dump.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Apollo Control, Houston here; 78 hours, 19 minutes into the flight.
We've had a few sporadic conversations with two very hard working pilots, actually I guess all the conversation has been with one, Jim Lovell, in the last few minutes.
Here's a collection of those conversations.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And that brings up to snuff with all the tape of this pass.
I think we're going to go back to live action, let's cut up there.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Our orbit this rev, sixty - shows a 62-[nautical] mile apogee [means apolune] and a 60.1-[nautical] mile perigee [means perilune], perigee occurring at 10 degrees south by 101 degrees east and apogee occurring at 10 degrees north by 78 west.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're ready for the - your updates, your PADs.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: I have updates, a map update for rev 5/6, and TEI-6 update.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: I've got the map update page now.
Why don't you give me that one?
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: LOS, 78:58:49; sunrise, 79:08:07; prime meridian, 79:14:30; AOS, 79:44:36; sunset, 80:21:05; IP-1, time of closest approach to target B-1, 80:09:08.
Now your two new control points that Jim gave us: control point number 1, acquisition, 79:10:32; control point number 2, acquisition, 79:23:14.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Before we read the big TEI update here, I'd like to give Jim, briefly, a procedure for P22.
When he comes to Noun 89, we request that he do a Verb 34, Enter.
Do not Proceed, and by so doing then, he will not incorporate the lat.
and longitude from his mark, and he will not change the reference value of the landing site, and we will solve this auto optics problem.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Let me see if I have this correct, Mike.
When flashing [Verb] 06 [Noun] 89 comes up with the latitude and longitude information, I will not Proceed but will go to Verb 34 and terminate.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Do a Verb 34, Enter, instead of a Proceed.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Houston, is this technique true for both the node control point auto optics onG-25?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: True for the code auto optics and the no landmark.
I'll Proceed instead of going on - or I'll use 34 instead Proceeding on 89.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Thank you, Jim, and I have the TEI-6 hour, when you're ready - or TEI number 6.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: I'm glad you're ready to copy TEI number 6.
I've got one last comment for Jim before you do so.
The Verb 89 - or correction - the Noun 89 we're talking about is the one that he gets after marking.
There are two Noun 89s, one prior to marking and one after, and our procedure references the Noun 89 after marking.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Thank you, and, Bill, you still ready to copy?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Are you with me so far?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: 3181.6, 3:02, 3162.4; 40, 269.9, 39.6; 033, down 05.4, left 2.1; plus 08.10, minus 165.00, 1296.8, 36222, 146:42:04; GDC Align remains the same, Sirus and Rigel; roll 129, pitch 155, yaw 010; ullage, 4 quads for 15 seconds.
Horizon on 6-degree line at TIGminus 3 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: The PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:The PAD is for an emergency burn to return to Earth at the end of Rev 6.Systems:The burn would be made using the SPS engine, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.CSM Weight (Noun 47):45,701 pounds (20,730 kg).Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):-0.40° and +1.57°.Time of ignition (Noun 33):81 hours, 21 minutes, 24.43 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, +3,177.6 (+968.5); Y, -82.3 (-25.1); Z, -136.5 (-41.6).

The large positive number in the X direction implies a large prograde component, essentially adding to their orbital velocity, exactly what would be expected from an escape manoeuvre.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 180°; Pitch, 16°; Yaw, 1°.

The desired spacecraft attitude is measured relative to the alignment of the guidance platform which itself has been aligned per theLOI-2 REFSMMAT.Expected apogee of resulting orbit (Noun 44):Not applicable.

Being initiated around the Moon, the apogee of the resulting orbit around Earth is too large to register on the computer.Expected perigee of resulting orbit (Noun 44):18.8 nautical miles (34.8 km).Delta-VT:3,181.6 fps (969.8 m/s).
The total sum of the three velocity components.Burn duration or burn time:3 minutes, 02 seconds.Delta-VC:3,162.4 fps.

This figure will be entered into the EMS to allow it to shut down the engine as a backup in case the G&N system fails to do so.Sextant star:Star 40 (Altair, or Alpha Aquilae) visible in sextant when shaft and trunnion angles are 269.9° and 39.6° respectively.Boresight star:Star 33 (Antares, or Alpha Scorpii).COAS Pitch Angle:Down 5.4°.COAS X Position Angle:Left 2.1°.Expected splashdown point (Noun 61):8.10° north, 165° west; which is in the mid-Pacific.Range to go at the 0.05 g event:1,296.8 nautical miles.
To set up their EMS (Entry Monitor System) before re-entry, the crew need to know the expected distance the CM would travel from the 0.05 g event to landing.
This figure will be decremented by the EMS based on signals from its own accelerometer.Expected velocity at the 0.05 g event:36,222 fps.
This is another entry for the EMS.

It is entered into the unit's Delta-V counter and will be decremented based on signals from its own accelerometer.Predicted GET of 0.05 g event:146 hours, 42 minutes and 4 seconds GET.GDC Align stars:Stars to be used for GDC Align purposes are Sirius and Rigel.GDC Align angles:Roll, 129°; pitch, 155°; yaw, 10°.There are two additional points given in the PAD.
An ullage burn of 15 seconds should be made by all four RCS quads to settle the contents of the half-empty SPS tanks prior to the burn.
This is to minimise the chance of helium gas being ingested when the engine ignites.
The Moon's horizon should be lined up on the rendezvous window's 6° line at the moment of ignition.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 39.6; 033, down 05.4, left 2.1; plus 08.10, minus 165.00; 1296.8, 36222, 146:42:04; same GDC Align, Sirius and Rigel, 129, 155, 010; 4 jet, 15 seconds; horizon, 6 degrees, TIGminus 3.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: On your ignition time, GETIis 81 hours, 081.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: As a matter of interest, these side windows are so hazy, that when the Sun shines on them, they just about - they're real poor for any visual observation or photography - heads up.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: The last state vector updates we sent you, Jim, was to the LM slots and you'll have to transfer that over to the CSM slots using Verb 47 Enter.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: Bill has got his tape recorder back, and we noticed during that last dump, it was all in low bit rate.
We wonder whether that was intentional or not?
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We didn't have much to say; we couldn't see out of the windows very well, Mike.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston here; and that's a pretty tired Jim Lovell we're hearing today.
Take it from somebody who's listened to him now for some 18 - 14 and 4 and 3 - 21 days [he is adding up the days Jim has spent in space on Gemini 7, Gemini 12 and Apollo 8 so far].
Here's some more conversation, I think he's just about to get a Go for Rev 6.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This next time around into the sunlight, we do not expect any problem with the primary evaporator.
If it does start drying out, we think it's best just to close the back-pressure valve, and there's no need to activate the secondary boiler.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: I guess the 60-degree limit will still hold then.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: We're suggesting you disregard the 60-degree limit; let it go ahead and rise up above 60.
There's no need to activate the secondary.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We just don't want to boil our IMU.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: Apollo 8, you're Go for the next lunar orbit rev.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: I'll read the book this time.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: We're 15 minutes to Loss of Signal.
We'll take the line down and bring you any additional comments that may occur between now and Loss of Signal.
This is Apollo Control, Houston at 78 hours, 43 minutes.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We have about 4½ minutes to LOS; and before we have LOS, we'd like your last PRD readout.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: The Commander's asleep; we'll get his when he wakes up.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: The LMP is still 6, point - correction, 0.64.
transcript.speaker: Anders

tokens: About 40 seconds to LOS, and everything's looking good down here.
transcript.speaker: Collins

tokens: We'll give it another try here.
transcript.speaker: Lovell
